speaker: interviewer
text: --Schwartz. You were talking about-- that you-- you remarried a second time. And his name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jay. Jay Schwartz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you wanted to talk a little bit about Denise, who was the nun that was responsible for your-- saving you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think there's two-- there's many important events that happened fairly recently that, even though I was open about my experiences, when I felt a person would care and listen or a group would care and listen, I might tell my story, but not to anybody. And so also-- so I was encouraged by-- by my husband, my son, and friends, and the lady, Thea, who had been cultural counselor in the Jewish orphanage and with whom I'm still in touch to inscribe my parents' name on the monument to the victims of war and deportation in Clichy, the working-class suburb of Paris where I grew up. And so I went to Paris. And I petitioned City Hall. And it was done in 1988. And I was also encouraged-- there was a lot of red tape in doing it. And I was also encouraged by Dr. Flo Kinsler and Dr. Sarah Moskovitz, who founded the group Child Survivors of the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: So around that time I joined the group, what happened is that I was on the mailing list of the second generation, and there was an-- there was an ad asking people who were child survivors if they wanted to join an encounter group. And so I called up. I believe I talked to Flo. And she got me in touch with [INAUDIBLE]. And so I joined the encounter group. So it-- I forgot how many weeks-- maybe 6 to 10 weeks of encountering once a week on Pico and Barrington. And at that encounter group, we had two psychologists-- Dr. Flo Kinsler and Dr. Sarah Moskovitz. And they told us that, at times, they don't agree-- which was all right with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this encounter was really [SOBBING] amazing. Because it was the first time that I found myself with people under similar circumstances. And the group had people who went into hiding, like me, and survivors of the death camps. So in a sense, it was a traumatic experience, but it was catharsis. It was beneficial to all of us. And so I became active. I joined in all the activities. And eventually, Dr. Sarah Moskovitz became my personal psychotherapist. And she has helped me a lot through the different crises in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Um--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, and I need to mention-- when I saw-- am I out of time?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. I just don't know why that phone is ringing. But, OK. [LAUGH] No, you're not. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw the movie I went with a friend, and we cried on each other's shoulder. And I came out of the movie, wanting to know which list I was on-- another Schindler's. Who could it be? And then I saw a French movie called with Shakespeare's theme, where people whom we thought dead are resurrected. Now, whether it takes place in our imagination or our longing, or some people reappear out of nowhere is not clear. But I had a very strong longing for the past to be brought to me again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so within a week or two of seeing both movies, I got a letter from [INAUDIBLE], one of the ladies who had been a girl in the same Jewish orphanage where I was. And she sent me a little letter with a photocopy of a brochure. And she said, I'm a member of the Hidden Children in Paris by Serge Klarsfeld. The latest bulletin had a list of 150 people. And guess whose name is on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my name was on it. And she says, I thought you might be interested. And so when I saw the list, I was overwhelmed. And I found out whose list I was on. I was on the list of Deniz Pola Aguanish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these were 150 children. She asked them-- she asked us to be in touch with her, and I wrote to her. And she wrote back. And she invited-- I said I would visit France that summer, two years ago. And she invited me to spend a week with her, at her home in the region of Grenoble. And so I spent a wonderful week with her. And she told me all kinds of wonderful things about herself. And when I walked in that home, which was her parents' home, I knew I had been there before. And she says, well, this home was added. And this was remodeled. And I said, uh-uh. I said, these rooms do nothing to me. It's these old rooms. I know I've been there. She laughed. She says, well, my parents' home was called the Jewish Nest. And there were rumors. And she told me wonderful stories, how she had saved-- she and the Resistance and Catholic and Jews had saved the lives of 650 Jewish children and countless thousands of refugees and that some Jews were hidden in the-- where the bell is of the church. And she knew they were religious Jews. She would bring them religious objects, where they could lit candles-- recite prayers. And then in the morning or evening, they would empty the bucket. And they were hidden there. And-- and she has been honored by Yad Vashem. They planted a tree in her honor. And she showed me that video. She was honored by the French and Israeli ambassador. And her name is written on the walls of the museum, the Holocaust Museum in Washington, DC. She's a tiny woman. I met her. She was 81, deeply religious. I was told that she was a nun during the war. She left the convent and married and has a daughter with grandchildren. And a remarkable person, full of love and caring, and she came from a long line of doctors, surgeons, social workers, nurses. And her whole life she spent as a nurse and social worker. And-- and 16 years ago, her-- she-- she had a brain tumor. And she was hospitalized. And meanwhile, her husband had a heart condition. And they operated, they saved her life. But on the day she was released from the hospital, she found out [SOBBING] her husband had died. So the next day she went to her funeral. And she said to me, the Lord has always taken care of me, and he always will. And she told-- now, I also talked to Colette on the phone. I found out information--

speaker: interviewer
text: And Colette is the woman who brought you to-- to-- to Deniz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe she is. She was one of the social workers with Deniz. And she told me the story that one time she was going with a briefcase, in Grenoble. And all the names of the children, where they were at and refugees, were in that briefcase. But they were covered with cookies and candies and all that. And she was arrested by the French police or the Gestapo. They opened the briefcase, ate a cookie, and told her to go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked-- I said, did you meet-- did you meet and were told what to do? She says, no, because they had met and the famously-- the Resistance was tortured and murdered from Lyon. And she says, from that moment on, they met in the shadow. And they-- they never knew-- they couldn't guarantee that if they were arrested they wouldn't talk. And they all carry the poison capsule in case they would be arrested. Including herself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And everything was done in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I want to thank you for sharing your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you-- is there anything else you'd like to add, or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, I want to sing this song. And I want to introduce my son and go through those photos and documents. Oh, I just want to mention that, after joining the Hidden Child Survivors 50 years later, I started looking for what had happened to my parents. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --I was encouraged. And so it took me 50 years to found out-- to find out that they had died in Auschwitz. And I have the documents and the date--

speaker: interviewer
text: And we can look at the documents.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of their deaths.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we'll photograph them later. Is there anything else that you'd like to add that you haven't said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I just want to say that coming to America or everything-- I've-- I've often endured persecution and anti-Semitism, you know. I'm aware of hardship in my life, in other people's lives. So I feel very strongly that we have to be vigilant. We have to fight for the oppressed. Somehow the torch must be carried on and to do what's right in our lives, not to close our eyes or turn away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you. I appreciate those words of wisdom. And we'll continue with the introducing your son and the photographs. Maya, I'd like you to introduce us to your son, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my wonderful son, Michael Samuel Charnes. And he's a wonderful musician and teacher and a gorgeous human being and soul mate. And I know [SOBBING] my parents would be very proud of him. And he has inherited their talent and their level of justice and love of music and literature.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'd like to sing for you the famous-- 'Le Chant des Marais.' It's known-- it's known in English as the 'Peat Bog Soldier's Song.' It was the concentration-camp song for Jews and non-Jews-- the victims of Nazism. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: 'We are in a camp, surrounded by swamps, barbed wires, soldiers, guns. All we hear are the screams and the heavy boots marching on and the screams. And all we see is blood and death around us. There are no birds who ever sing. But one day in our life, spring will bloom again. Freedom, beloved freedom, I will say you are mine.' I will sing it in French. [MUSIC - 'LE CHANT DES MARAIS'] [INAUDIBLE] [SINGING IN FRENCH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to say the last chorus is 'Oh, land, finally free, where we can be born once more and love and love.'

speaker: interviewer
text: That was beautiful. That was really beautiful. Would you like to say anything-- have anything to add, that you would like to add about your mother's life or story?

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing I'd like to say-- she's told her story pretty well. I wouldn't have anything to add, except [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what it's left for me is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some people going through all that suffering, you know, become hardened and cynical or take that out on others. And for my mother, somehow through all that has become an extremely generous and loving person. And that's-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that generosity and that compassionate nature and that model of conscience is-- has been the biggest strength for me in my life. And I-- I aspire to be like her in that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Maya, tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: The one on the back of the chair-- incidentally, both photos were given to me by my uncle in Israel. And Michael visited him, I think, in 1962. We took a trip to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took those two photos out of his album and gave them to me. And his name is Naithan. And that's little Nathan when he was a little boy. And that's my grandmother-- In the center, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the center. And that's my mom. And this picture was taken in the '20s when they first arrived in Palestine, what was to become later Israel. And the next picture--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait-- we have--

speaker: interviewee
text: I go ahead?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This next picture-- it was taken in 1929. And my parents were either married or they were engaged, or-- anyway, they were activists in the left-wing movement. And with them is a friend. His name at the time was Israel Friedrik. And there's a wonderful story, because I was to run into him at a party, not knowing. 25 years after I came to America, Michael and I sang at a Jewish party. And here he was. So here he is, in that picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1929. And [INAUDIBLE], do you want to read in Yiddish and translate what it says?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was taken in what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right, tell us about the top picture, there.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [CRYING] This represents life before the war. And we were a happy family. I was four and five-- four or five. And they said I was an adorable-looking Shirley Temple, always singing, bursting into song. And I remember my mother and her beautiful black dress. And this brooch I remember very well. So this was before the war. And we are a happy, united family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken in-- in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris. And in those days, people went to a studio and posed for a photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Oh, now we're going to look at the bottom picture with-- right directly below it. Tell us about that picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember this-- I remember taking the picture. We went-- the three of us went to the studio. It was in 1939. I was seven years old. My father was on a furlough from the army. And the dress that you see was a purple dress. It was made by my mother's girlfriend Anna, who was a wonderful seamstress and embroidered things for me. And the topcoat was made by Esther, another girlfriend of my mama, who made the same coat for her son Daniel and for me. And it-- it was a plaid coat with a black velvet collar. And I remember all of us combing our hair, posing. This picture is of particular significance, because my mother mailed it to me from the concentration camp near Bordeaux-Merignac. And so these are her last words written to me in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll get to the back. What about the next photo--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to the-- to the-- --to the left of that one?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very painful memory. And that-- my father mailed to us this photo from his concentration camp in Beaune-la-Rolande.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your father, in that photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is in the middle. He's the tall guy with the mustache.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want me to talk about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, the monument. Let's look at the big-- the big version of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. In-- in-- in Port de Clichy, Mairie Clichy, there is a monument to the victims of war and deportation. And it lists Auschwitz. And I petitioned the authority, with my son, husband's encouragement and dear, dear friend Sarah. And they-- City Hall put my parents' name on the monument in 1988. And Michael and I went there a few years ago. And we sang a song. We recited a poem. We remembered my beloved parents [SOBBING] and all our friends who perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you can see their names are engraved on there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I like the feeling of [INAUDIBLE] flowers. And Dr. Sarah Moskovitz and her husband, they went to Clichy. And her husband took these two beautiful photographs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK. Maya, this is the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you read the first one and tell us-- just tell us what it says, in English, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My father wrote, 'as a-- as a remembrance of my being interned in front of my castle, after seven months' stay in the camp of Beaune-La-Rolande, to my beloved Rachel and to my love Maya, I am sending you this photo. Let us hope that better days will come and that our separation will soon be ended and that the day of joy and happiness will also be for us. Your Samuel, Beaune-la-Rolande.' It's dated December 18, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK. Maya, this is this postcard from your mother. If you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's dated Merignac, the camp, on the-- so it'd be May-- May 24, 1942. 'My dear Maya, [SOBBING] I send you, as a remembrance, the photo of mama and papa. Let's hope we will see each other soon. Your mama, Rachel.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Speak about this picture, here.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken by a professional photographer. And I was getting ready to present a program in Berkeley called The History of France Through-- Through Songs. And he liked the theme of a glass of wine and bread. And I was 30, in that picture, and I kind of like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And now, at the bottom picture, there-- you with your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a proud moment in my life. My son is 14, and it's his bar mitzvah. And I'm so glad he could have that Jewish education and the celebration--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 13.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of his coming of age. And that's proud mother and loving friends and loved ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was 14--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 13.

speaker: interviewee
text: 13, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And taken in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Thank you. OK, tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a happy picture. Shortly after we were married, my husband Jay and I-- this was 26 years ago. And we went to Hawaii for our honeymoon. And we are both wearing a lovely Hawaiian outfit. And at that moment, we were very happy. And-- and Jay has been a very good father to my son Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Maya, tell us about this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is a happy moment in my life. Because I had a chance to say thank you to Deniz, who saved my life and the lives of other children and who risked her life to save mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is Deniz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Deniz is the lady with the white blouse and-- shirt and white shoes. And at the time, two years ago, she was 81. And she took me on a pilgrimage to the Sisters of the Good Shepherd in Grenoble and the Sister of Notre-Dame de Sion. And in the middle is another Deniz. And she was the singing nun of the Convent of the Good Shepherd. They both were nuns, but they left the convent later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Deniz, the singing nun, when I first saw her, I said, you're Deniz? I was in your children's choir. And she burst into song. And both of them are just full of love. And it was quite a moving pilgrimage for me to thank them personally-- And this was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --50 years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1994, this picture was taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The last person--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are-- your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maya Schwartz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. OK. Tell us about this particular photograph, Maya.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went on the pilgrimage. We had lunch at the Convent at the Good Shepherd-- and Notre-Dame de Sion. And Deniz took us on a tour of the old convent. And this is the chapel. And I sang in the children's choir. And I always enjoyed it. It was warm and lovely. And I always enjoyed the rituals of the mass. And it was a warm place. The rest of the place was freezing. And the convent is very little now. There is no longer a cloister. They no longer take care of children. They take care of sick, old people. And most of it has been sold. All that remains is the eating room, [FRENCH], and the chapel and maybe the dormitories. And the nuns and some of the ladies-- children who decided to live there-- are still active, in their 80s and 90s-- ironing, washing, taking care of people. They go on with their life of charity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And Maya, please tell us about the photograph of this woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: This lady is Soeur Marie-Joseph of the order of Notre-Dame de Sion. I understand the order will be honored by Yad Vashem, because as an order they saved the lives of thousands of children. And this nun, we had lunch with her. She's in her 90s. And she knew-- she knew what went on. And she told all kinds of stories about how they hid people, how some people were found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in 1994?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Yes, yes, taken in 1994 when Deniz took me to a pilgrimage to-- and I met her. So this-- this lady knows--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] And her name, again, is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soeur Marie-Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's in her 90s, very active.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
